S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Choose examples that teach what you actually intend to demonstrate
· Label examples so Claude knows which features to copy and which to ignore
· Use counterexamples to prevent the failure modes that unlabeled examples often create
· Control voice and style without losing substance
Keep examples from overfitting producing outputs that mimic surface features while missing the underlying task
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: HOW EXAMPLES WORK: IN-CONTEXT LEARNING
blocks: When you provide an example in a prompt, you are using a mechanism researchers call few-shot prompting or in-context lea
How Examples Work: In-Context Learning — A single example is one-shot prompting. Two or more examples are few-shot prompting. Zero examples — just a task — is ze

data.title: WHAT EXAMPLES TEACH (BESIDES WHAT YOU THINK).
blocks: An example teaches format, level, tone, reasoning pattern, length, vocabulary register, and rhetorical approach — often
What Examples Teach (Besides What You Th — A research note pasted as an example teaches Claude to hedge like a researcher. A corporate memo teaches Claude to use p

S05.meta: // SLIDE 05 — BEFORE AND AFTER: ANNOTATING A

data.title: BEFORE AND AFTER: ANNOTATING AN EXAMPLE
blocks: · It named which features to copy (sentence rhythm, tense, emotional register)
· It excluded which features not to copy (subject matter)
· It stated the target subject matter directly

data.title: COUNTEREXAMPLES: TEACHING BY SHOWING WHAT TO AVOID
blocks: Tone failures
Format drift
Overfitting prevention

data.title: STYLE TRANSFER: VOICE SAMPLES AND THEIR LIMITS
blocks: · Maintaining a consistent authorial voice across multiple documents
· Adapting content for a different audience while preserving brand register
· Producing multiple versions of the same content in different registers

data.title: FEW-SHOT EXTRACTION: WHEN FORMAT CONSISTENCY MATTERS MOST
blocks: 18% fewer stress-related absences
18% among remote financial services workers (2022 study)

S09.meta: // SLIDE 09 — STYLE GUIDES AS REUSABLE CONTE

data.title: STYLE GUIDES AS REUSABLE CONTEXT.
blocks: A style guide pasted as context is a special form of few-shot example: it provides multiple rules and examples at once,
Style Guides as Reusable Context — The practical limit is length. Including a twenty-page style guide in every prompt is expensive in tokens and may bury o

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE.
blocks: Substance check Does the output contain accurate information, or did it copy the form of the example while inventing the content?
Scope check Did Claude stay on the stated subject, or did it drift toward the example's subject matter?
Style fidelity check Does the output match the named features of the style — not just look vaguely similar to the example?
Overfit check Would this output make sense to a reader who had not seen the example, or does it depend on implicit example-knowledge t

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Using an example without annotating it.
Using an example from the wrong register.
Using too many examples when one is enough.
Expecting style to substitute for substance.
Reproducing copyrighted examples at length without consideration.
